---
title: "1.3 Expressions and Output"
subtitle: "Part 1: Output, Literals, and Escape Sequences"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Output

### Two methods for screen output

Java has two different methods to print output to the screen:

::: {.tight-list}
- **`System.out.println(value)`** prints the value followed by a new line (`ln`).
- **`System.out.print(value)`** prints the value without advancing to the next line.
:::

## String Literals

### Characters inside double quotes

`System.out.println("Hi there!");` prints out the characters between the first `"` and the second `"` followed by a new line.

`"Hi there!"` is called a <span class="term">string literal</span> (字符串字面量), which is zero to many characters enclosed in starting and ending double quotes.

A <span class="term">literal</span> (字面量) is the code representation of a fixed value, which can be a string or a numerical value.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: printCommands`

Textbook prompt: Run this code to see the output below it.

How would you change it to print the `!` on the same line as `Hi there` while keeping all three print statements?

## Code Window

### `activecode:: printCommands`

```java
public class MyClass
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        System.out.print("Hi ");
        System.out.println("there");
        System.out.print("!");
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: printCommands`

Runestone expects one output line:

```text
Hi there!
```

The line-count test expects `1 output line`.

## Escape Sequences

### Printing special characters

What if you wanted to print out a double quote `"` character?

Since the double quote `"` is a special character with meaning in Java, we put in a backslash in front of the quote to signal that we want just the character.

This is called a <span class="term">backslash escape sequence</span> (反斜杠转义序列).

## Escape Sequences

### AP CSA escape sequences

If you wanted to print out a backslash, you would have to backslash it too in order to escape its special meaning.

Another useful backslashed character is backslash `\n`, which will put in a newline.

AP CSA escape sequences here:

::: {.tight-list}
- `\"`
- `\\`
- `\n`
:::

## Code Task

### `activecode:: bhescape`

Textbook prompt: Here are the escape sequences that may be used in the AP course.

Run the code and inspect how the string is printed.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: bhescape`

```java
public class TestEscape
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        String message =
                "Here is a backslash quote \" "
                        + " and a backslashed backslash (\\) "
                        + "Backslash n \n prints out a new line.";
        System.out.println(message);
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: bhescape`

Runestone checks that `main` runs and prints the message.

The teaching focus is recognizing that these are one string value split across concatenated source lines:

::: {.tight-list}
- `\"` prints a quote character.
- `\\` prints a backslash character.
- `\n` creates a new output line.
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- distinguish `System.out.println(value)` from `System.out.print(value)`
- explain that `println` advances to a new line and `print` does not
- define <span class="term">literal</span> as the code representation of a fixed value
- define <span class="term">string literal</span> as characters inside double quotes
- identify `\"`, `\\`, and `\n` as AP CSA escape sequences
:::

## End

### Continue to Part 2

[Next: Arithmetic and compound expressions →](1-3-part-2-arithmetic-and-compound-expressions.html)
